serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Fried Hokkien Mee as typically served at a Singapore hawker centre, weighing approximately 400 g.

nutrient_source.title: Hokkien Mee (Fried) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists 'Hokkien Mee (Fried)' as a distinct hawker dish entry. Per-100 g values are taken directly from that entry. The restaurant-specific 'Signature' designation does not alter the nutritional reference; the HPB entry represents the canonical Singapore hawker preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — noodle dishes and mixed grain-based meals
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore Fried Hokkien Mee in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 49 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: yellow wheat noodles (Hokkien-style) in a mixed stir-fried preparation, which the database records at GI ~47–52 (mean 49). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (49 × 72.8) / 100 ≈ 35.7, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 18.2 × 4 = 72.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (480 mg per 100 g; approximately 1920 mg per standard 400 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Fried Hokkien Mee due to its very high sodium load (~1920 mg per serving), which represents approximately 83% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg; requesting reduced soy sauce and omitting sambal can partially mitigate sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (49) but high glycemic load per serving (~35.7) due to large noodle portion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the GI of Fried Hokkien Mee is in the low range (49), the high carbohydrate load per standard serving (~72.8 g) produces a high glycemic load of approximately 35.7, which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion (150–200 g) and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from lard and pork belly, combined with high sodium and dietary cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fried Hokkien Mee contains saturated fat (~1.8 g per 100 g, ~7.2 g per serving) and cholesterol (~72 mg per 100 g) from lard and pork belly, alongside high sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, request preparation without lard, and consume this dish infrequently as part of a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Hokkien Mee (福建炒面) is a quintessential Singapore hawker dish originating from Hokkien Chinese immigrants who settled in Singapore and Malaysia. It consists of thick yellow wheat noodles and thin rice vermicelli stir-fried together in a rich, flavourful prawn-and-pork stock until the noodles absorb the broth and develop a slightly caramelised, umami-laden coating. The dish is typically cooked over high heat in a wok with lard, garlic, prawns, squid, sliced pork belly, and egg, then finished with dark soy sauce. It is served with sambal chilli, calamansi lime, and sometimes crispy lard bits (pork cracklings) on the side. The texture is soft and slightly sticky from the braising liquid, with a smoky wok-hei aroma when prepared over charcoal or high-flame gas. Nutritionally, a standard 400 g hawker serving provides approximately 592 kcal, 31 g protein, 20.8 g fat, and 72.8 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is high at approximately 1920 mg per serving, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 49 (low), but the glycemic load per serving is high (~35.7) due to the large noodle portion, which is clinically relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with shellfish, mollusc, egg, or gluten allergies. It is not halal-certified in most traditional preparations due to the use of lard and pork.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo under the 'Hokkien Mee (Fried)' entry, which is an exact match for the dish category. The 'Signature' restaurant designation does not have a separate nutritional profile; the HPB canonical hawker preparation is used as the reference. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on yellow wheat noodle stir-fry entries, as no direct GI measurement for Singapore Fried Hokkien Mee was found. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source entry for this dish. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source entry for this dish. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source entry for this dish. 4. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in the HPB source for this preparation. 5. GI is an analogous estimate (GI 49) based on yellow Hokkien-style wheat noodle stir-fry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Singapore Fried Hokkien Mee was identified. 6. The 'Signature' prefix in the restaurant menu name is a commercial descriptor and does not alter the nutritional reference used. 7. Halal status is false for traditional preparations using lard and pork; halal-certified variants exist but are not the canonical preparation. 8. Serving size of 400 g is based on typical Singapore hawker centre portion weight for this dish as referenced in HPB portion guidance.
